skills.skill: 8.1 Finding the Average Value of a Function on an Interval
skills.prompt: This topic covers finding the average value of a function for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-4.B, Unit 8, Topic 8.1). The average value of f on [a,b] is f_avg = (1/(b-a)) * integral from a to b of f(x)dx. This generalizes the arithmetic mean to continuous functions: it is the height of the rectangle with base (b-a) that has the same area as the region under f. The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals guarantees that if f is continuous on [a,b], there exists c in [a,b] such that f(c) = f_avg. Key concepts: computing the average value using FTC2; interpreting the average value in context (e.g., the average temperature over a time interval); distinguishing average value from average rate of change. Students should be able to set up and evaluate the integral, then divide by the interval length. AP exam note: average value questions appear regularly in both multiple-choice and free-response. Students must use the correct formula and interpret the result in context with units. Vocabulary: average value, Mean Value Theorem for Integrals, continuous, interval, integral, rectangle.

skills.skill: 8.2 Connecting Position, Velocity, and Acceleration of Functions Using Integrals
skills.prompt: This topic covers using integrals in particle motion for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-4.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.2). If a(t) is acceleration, then v(t) = v(t0) + integral from t0 to t of a(s)ds; if v(t) is velocity, then s(t) = s(t0) + integral from t0 to t of v(s)ds. Key concepts: displacement = integral from a to b of v(t)dt (net change in position, can be negative); total distance traveled = integral from a to b of |v(t)|dt (always nonnegative, accounts for direction changes); position at time t is initial position plus displacement. To compute total distance, find where v(t) = 0 (direction changes), split the integral, and take absolute values. Students should connect this to the Net Change Theorem: the integral of a rate of change gives the net change in the quantity. AP exam note: distinguishing displacement from total distance is crucial. Free-response questions frequently ask for both. Students must show work for finding direction changes. Vocabulary: displacement, total distance, velocity, acceleration, position, Net Change Theorem, direction change, absolute value.

skills.skill: 8.3 Using Accumulation Functions and Definite Integrals in Applied Contexts
skills.prompt: This topic covers applying definite integrals in real-world contexts for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-4.D, CHA-4.E, Unit 8, Topic 8.3). When R(t) represents a rate (gallons per minute, people per hour, etc.), the integral from a to b of R(t)dt gives the total accumulated quantity over [a,b]. Key applications: total water flowing into/out of a tank, total number of customers arriving, net change in population, total cost from a marginal cost function. For problems involving both inflow and outflow rates, the net change is the integral of (inflow rate - outflow rate). Students must interpret results in context with correct units: if R is in gallons/minute and t is in minutes, the integral is in gallons. The accumulation function F(t) = F(a) + integral from a to t of R(s)ds tracks the running total. AP exam note: applied integral problems are a staple of free-response sections. Students must set up integrals correctly from contextual descriptions and interpret results clearly. Vocabulary: accumulation, net change, inflow, outflow, rate function, total quantity, units, context.

skills.skill: 8.4 Finding the Area Between Curves Expressed as Functions of x
skills.prompt: This topic covers finding the area between curves as functions of x for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-5.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.4). The area between y = f(x) (top) and y = g(x) (bottom) from x = a to x = b is A = integral from a to b of [f(x) - g(x)]dx, where f(x) >= g(x) on [a,b]. Key steps: (1) find intersection points by solving f(x) = g(x) to determine the limits of integration; (2) determine which function is on top in each interval; (3) set up and evaluate the integral of (top - bottom). If the curves cross within the interval, split into subintervals where each function is on top. Students should sketch the region to visualize which function is above the other. Area is always positive, so always subtract the lower function from the upper. AP exam note: area between curves is commonly tested. Students must clearly identify the upper and lower functions and find correct intersection points. Vocabulary: area between curves, upper function, lower function, intersection points, limits of integration, top minus bottom.

skills.skill: 8.5 Finding the Area Between Curves Expressed as Functions of y
skills.prompt: This topic covers finding the area between curves expressed as functions of y for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-5.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.5). When curves are more naturally expressed as x = f(y) and x = g(y), integrate with respect to y: A = integral from c to d of [f(y) - g(y)]dy, where f(y) is the rightmost function and g(y) is the leftmost, and y ranges from c to d. Key concepts: this approach is preferable when horizontal slicing simplifies the setup (e.g., when the curves are not functions of x or when integrating with respect to x would require splitting into multiple integrals). Students should determine which function is on the right (larger x-value) and integrate (right - left). The limits c and d are y-values of intersection points. AP exam note: students should be able to choose between integrating with respect to x or y based on which is simpler. Both approaches must yield the same area. Vocabulary: area between curves, functions of y, horizontal slicing, right minus left, integrate with respect to y, intersection.

skills.skill: 8.6 Finding the Area Between Curves That Intersect at More Than Two Points
skills.prompt: This topic covers area between curves with multiple intersection points for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-5.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.6). When two curves intersect at more than two points, the region between them consists of multiple sub-regions. The total area requires: (1) finding all intersection points; (2) determining which function is on top in each sub-interval; (3) integrating (top - bottom) over each sub-interval separately; (4) adding the absolute values. Alternatively, the total area = integral of |f(x) - g(x)|dx over the full interval, which requires splitting at each crossing point. Key concept: the area is always nonnegative, so if the upper and lower functions switch, each sub-region must be handled separately. Students should sketch the curves to identify all regions and which function dominates in each. AP exam note: these problems test careful setup and attention to sign changes. Students must find all intersection points and handle each region correctly. Vocabulary: multiple intersections, sub-regions, crossing points, piecewise integration, total area, absolute value.

skills.skill: 8.7 Volumes with Cross Sections: Squares and Rectangles
skills.prompt: This topic covers computing volumes using known cross sections for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-5.B, Unit 8, Topic 8.7). A solid has a known base region in the xy-plane, and cross sections perpendicular to an axis have a specified shape. For cross sections perpendicular to the x-axis, V = integral from a to b of A(x)dx, where A(x) is the area of the cross section at position x. For square cross sections with side length s(x): A(x) = [s(x)]^2 where s(x) is typically the distance between the curves defining the base (top - bottom). For rectangular cross sections with height proportional to the base: A(x) = s(x)*h(x). Key steps: (1) identify the base region and the cross-section shape; (2) express the side length or dimensions in terms of x (or y); (3) write A(x) and integrate. Students should visualize the 3D solid by imagining stacking cross sections. AP exam note: cross-section volume problems are common free-response questions. Students must clearly express A(x) and set up the integral. Vocabulary: cross section, perpendicular, base region, square, rectangle, side length, volume integral.

skills.skill: 8.8 Volumes with Cross Sections: Triangles and Semicircles
skills.prompt: This topic covers volumes with triangular and semicircular cross sections for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-5.B, Unit 8, Topic 8.8). Building on Topic 8.7, the cross sections perpendicular to an axis are now triangles or semicircles. For equilateral triangle cross sections with side s(x): A(x) = (sqrt(3)/4)*[s(x)]^2. For isosceles right triangle cross sections with leg s(x): A(x) = (1/2)*[s(x)]^2. For semicircular cross sections with diameter s(x): A(x) = (pi/8)*[s(x)]^2, since radius = s(x)/2 and the area of a semicircle is (1/2)*pi*r^2. The base region determines s(x), usually the vertical distance between two curves. Volume = integral from a to b of A(x)dx. Key concept: the cross-section shape determines the formula for A(x); the integration process is the same regardless of shape. AP exam note: students must know the area formulas for equilateral triangles, isosceles right triangles, and semicircles. Careful setup of s(x) is critical. Vocabulary: cross section, equilateral triangle, isosceles right triangle, semicircle, area formula, diameter, volume.

skills.skill: 8.9 Volume with Disc Method: Revolving Around the x- or y-Axis
skills.prompt: This topic covers the disc method for solids of revolution for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-5.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.9). When a region is revolved around an axis and the resulting solid has no hole, use the disc method. For revolution around the x-axis: V = integral from a to b of pi*[f(x)]^2 dx, where f(x) is the distance from the curve to the axis of rotation. For revolution around the y-axis: V = integral from c to d of pi*[g(y)]^2 dy. Each cross section perpendicular to the axis of rotation is a disc (circle) with radius equal to the function value. Key concepts: the radius is always the distance from the curve to the axis of rotation; square the radius and multiply by pi to get the cross-sectional area; integrate along the axis of rotation. Students should sketch the region and axis to correctly identify the radius function. AP exam note: disc method problems are common. The most frequent error is forgetting to square the function or forgetting pi. Vocabulary: disc method, solid of revolution, axis of rotation, radius, cross-sectional area, revolve.

skills.skill: 8.10 Volume with Disc Method: Revolving Around Other Axes
skills.prompt: This topic covers the disc method for revolution around axes other than the coordinate axes for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-5.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.10). When revolving around y = k (horizontal line) or x = k (vertical line), the radius of each disc is the distance from the curve to the axis: |f(x) - k| for horizontal axes or |g(y) - k| for vertical axes. Key concept: always compute the radius as the distance between the curve and the axis of rotation, not just the function value. For example, revolving y = sqrt(x) around y = 3: radius = 3 - sqrt(x) (if the curve is below the axis) or sqrt(x) - 3 (if above). Students must carefully determine whether to subtract the function from the axis value or vice versa by considering the geometry. The integral becomes V = pi * integral of [radius]^2 dx (or dy). AP exam note: revolution around non-standard axes is frequently tested. Students should draw diagrams showing the axis and identify the radius correctly. Vocabulary: disc method, non-standard axis, distance from curve to axis, radius, revolution, horizontal line, vertical line.

skills.skill: 8.11 Volume with Washer Method: Revolving Around the x- or y-Axis
skills.prompt: This topic covers the washer method for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-5.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.11). When revolving a region bounded by two curves around an axis, the cross section is a washer (annulus) with outer radius R and inner radius r. The volume is V = pi * integral from a to b of [R(x)^2 - r(x)^2]dx for revolution around the x-axis. The outer radius R is the distance from the farther curve to the axis, and the inner radius r is the distance from the closer curve to the axis. Key concepts: the washer method is the disc method with a hole cut out; always subtract the inner radius squared from the outer radius squared (do NOT square the difference); identify outer and inner radii by determining which curve is farther from the axis. For revolution around the y-axis, integrate with respect to y. AP exam note: a common error is computing pi*integral of (R-r)^2 instead of pi*integral of (R^2 - r^2). Students should clearly label R and r in their setup. Vocabulary: washer method, outer radius, inner radius, annulus, solid of revolution, subtract squares.

skills.skill: 8.12 Volume with Washer Method: Revolving Around Other Axes
skills.prompt: This topic covers the washer method for revolution around non-standard axes for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-5.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.12). When revolving around y = k or x = k (not the coordinate axes), the outer and inner radii are distances from the curves to the axis of rotation. For revolution around y = k: R(x) = |f(x) - k| (farther curve) and r(x) = |g(x) - k| (closer curve). Key concept: carefully determine which curve is the outer boundary (farther from axis) and which is the inner boundary (closer to axis) by sketching the region and axis. The setup requires thoughtful geometric reasoning; drawing a representative rectangle perpendicular to the axis and rotating it helps identify the radii. Volume = pi * integral of [R^2 - r^2] dx (or dy). Students should verify their radii by checking at specific x-values. AP exam note: this combines the challenges of non-standard axes and the washer method. Meticulous setup with clearly labeled radii is essential for full credit. Vocabulary: washer method, non-standard axis, outer radius, inner radius, distance, revolution, representative rectangle.

skills.skill: 8.13 The Arc Length of a Smooth, Planar Curve and Distance Traveled
skills.prompt: This topic covers arc length for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-6.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.13). The arc length of a smooth curve y = f(x) from x = a to x = b is L = integral from a to b of sqrt(1 + [f'(x)]^2) dx. This formula comes from summing infinitesimal hypotenuses: ds = sqrt(dx^2 + dy^2) = sqrt(1 + (dy/dx)^2) dx. For parametric curves x = x(t), y = y(t) from t = a to t = b: L = integral from a to b of sqrt([x'(t)]^2 + [y'(t)]^2) dt. The distance traveled by a particle is the arc length of its path, which equals the integral of speed: integral of sqrt(x'(t)^2 + y'(t)^2) dt. Key concepts: arc length requires the curve to be smooth (continuous derivative); the integrand is always at least 1 (since sqrt(1 + [f']^2) >= 1); most arc length integrals cannot be evaluated in closed form and may require numerical methods. AP exam note: students should set up arc length integrals and evaluate them using a calculator when needed. Vocabulary: arc length, smooth curve, integrand, speed, distance traveled, parametric, Pythagorean theorem.
